facts: The patient's name is Betty.
The patient is frustrated with their asthma.
The patient experiences coughing and wheezing.
The patient's asthma is triggered by getting sick with a cold.
The patient's asthma is also triggered by their aunt's cat.
The patient tries to avoid their aunt's house due to the cat.
The patient has not had an issue since avoiding their aunt's house.
The patient experiences breathing problems every month or every other month.
The patient has had these breathing problems for the past six months.
The patient often visits urgent care for their breathing problems.
The patient's primary care provider or urgent care provider sometimes gives them antibiotics.
The patient's primary care provider or urgent care provider sometimes gives them steroids.
The patient has visited the emergency department for their asthma once.
The patient has two inhalers for their asthma.
The patient primarily uses one inhaler, a blue one.
The blue inhaler is a Ventolin inhaler.
The patient uses the Ventolin inhaler almost every day.
The patient uses the Ventolin inhaler at night a couple of times a week.
The patient takes two puffs of the Ventolin inhaler when using it.
The Ventolin inhaler helps the patient.
The patient was hospitalized for asthma once when they were around eight years old.
The patient has had several ER and urgent care visits in the past year.
The patient is often started on prednisone during these visits.
The patient sometimes takes a prednisone dose pack.
The patient sometimes takes a high dose of prednisone over five days.
The patient sometimes takes a tapering dose of prednisone over nine or ten days.
The patient's last asthma exacerbation was about a month ago.
The patient enjoys walking and hiking.
The patient does not have seasonal allergies.
The patient is careful when hiking due to their coughing and wheezing.
The patient is looking forward to hiking again when their asthma improves.
The patient has never smoked cigarettes.
The patient does not smoke marijuana.
The patient does not vape.
The patient is sometimes exposed to secondhand smoke in bars.
The patient tries to avoid bars due to the smoke.
The doctor performed a physical exam on the patient.
The patient's blood pressure is 128/82.
The patient's respiratory rate is 16.
The patient's oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
The patient's heart rate is regular.
The patient's lung exam showed diminished lung sounds and occasional expiratory wheezes.
The patient's neck exam was normal.
The patient's hands had strong bilateral pulses.
The patient did not have clubbing on their fingertips.
The patient had brisk capillary refill.
The patient's pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
The doctor diagnosed the patient with moderate intermittent asthma.
The doctor prescribed Flovent to the patient.
The Flovent dose is 110 micrograms.
The patient is to take one puff of Flovent twice a day.
Flovent is an inhaled steroid inhaler.
The doctor told the patient to continue using their Ventolin inhaler.
The patient is to use their Ventolin inhaler two puffs every four to six hours as needed.
The doctor is creating an asthma action plan for the patient.
The patient is to follow up with the doctor in a month.
The patient is to follow up with the doctor sooner if their symptoms worsen.
The doctor wants the patient to be consistent with their inhaled steroid inhaler.
The doctor wants the patient to use their albuterol inhaler one to two puffs during an acute exacerbation.
The patient is to wait and see how their breathing is before taking additional puffs of their albuterol inhaler.
